questions.question:
Consider an enterprise network with two Ethernet segments, a web server and a firewall, connected via three routers as shown below.

[Enterprise network diagram: Two Ethernet segments (left side), a Web Server and a Firewall (right side), interconnected through three routers. Each router interface connecting a distinct segment forms a separate subnet.]

What is the number of subnets inside the enterprise network?

A. 3
B. 12
C. 6
D. 8
questions.to_find: Number of subnets inside the enterprise network

understand.plain: An enterprise network has multiple routers each separating different network segments. Every link or LAN segment connected to a router interface is a separate subnet. We count all distinct subnets within the enterprise boundary.

keywords.explain: A logically distinct portion of a network sharing the same network address prefix, separated from other subnets by a router.
Each interface on a router connects to a different subnet; the number of subnets equals the number of router interfaces (counting distinct segments).
keywords.example: A router with 3 interfaces connects 3 different subnets.

keywords.explain: A shared broadcast domain (LAN) that constitutes one subnet.
keywords.example: Two PCs connected through an Ethernet switch form one subnet.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='480' height='220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='5' y='5' width='470' height='210' rx='8' class='bg'/><rect x='20' y='30' width='80' height='30' rx='4' class='hi'/><text x='60' y='50' text-anchor='middle' class='tag class='bg'/><text x='180' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>R1</text><rect x='250' y='65' width='60' height='30' rx='4' class='bg'/><text x='280' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>R2</text><rect x='350' y='65' width='60' height='30' rx='4' class='bg'/><text x='380' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>R3</text><rect x='390' y='30' width='70' height='25' rx='4' class='bg'/><text x='425' y='47' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>WebSvr</text><rect x='390' y='140' width='70' height='25' rx='4' class='bg'/><text x='425' y='157' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Firewall</text><line x1='100' y1='45' x2='150' y2='80' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='100' y1='115' x2='150' y2='80' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='210' y1='80' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='410' y1='80' x2='410' y2='140' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='240' y='185' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>6 subnets: ETH1, ETH2, R1-R2 link, R2-R3 link, WebSvr seg, Firewall seg</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Enterprise network with two Ethernet segments on the left feeding into Router R1, which connects to R2 via a point-to-point link, R2 connects to R3, and R3 connects to the web server and firewall subnets on the right.

given.aim: Count the total number of subnets inside the enterprise network formed by 3 routers, 2 Ethernet segments, a web server, and a firewall.

terms.meaning: Two LAN segments each forming one subnet.
terms.example: ETH-Seg1 and ETH-Seg2 are subnets 1 and 2.
terms.connects: Directly add 2 to the subnet count.

terms.meaning: Each link between two routers (R1-R2, R2-R3) forms a separate subnet.
terms.example: The R1-R2 serial link is subnet 3.
terms.connects: Adds 2 more subnets for the two inter-router links.

terms.meaning: Each of these devices sits on its own subnet connected to a router interface.
terms.example: Web server on one subnet (subnet 5), firewall on another (subnet 6).
terms.connects: Adds 2 more to the count, giving total of 6.

given.plan: Count every distinct network segment: the 2 Ethernet LANs, the 2 point-to-point router-to-router links, and the 2 host subnets (web server and firewall). Sum = 6.

steps.title: Identify LAN segments
steps.apply: Two Ethernet segments are present on the left side of the network, each forming one subnet. Count = 2.
steps.note: Each shared Ethernet segment is one broadcast domain = one subnet.

steps.title: Count inter-router links
steps.apply: Three routers form two point-to-point links: R1-R2 and R2-R3. Each link is a separate subnet. Count += 2 = 4.
steps.note: Point-to-point WAN links between routers each constitute a /30 or similar subnet.

steps.title: Count server and firewall subnets
steps.apply: The web server and the firewall are each on their own subnet connected to router R3's interfaces. Count += 2 = 6.
steps.note: Dedicated subnets for the web server and firewall are standard enterprise practice.

steps.title: Total subnet count
steps.formula_raw: Total = ETH segments + router links + host subnets = 2 + 2 + 2 = 6

questions.formula_note: Subnet counting rule: each router interface connecting to a distinct network segment defines one subnet. Sum all distinct segments in the enterprise boundary.

questions.question:
Node X has a TCP connection open to node Y. The packets from X to Y go through an intermediate IP router R and an Ethernet switch S. Consider a packet sent from X to Y over this connection. Ethernet switch S is the first switch on the network path between X and Y.

Which of the following statements is/are TRUE about the destination IP and MAC addresses on this packet at the time it leaves X?

A. The destination IP address is the IP address of R
B. The destination IP address is the IP address of Y
C. The destination MAC address is the MAC address of S
D. The destination MAC address is the MAC address of Y
questions.answer: B. The destination IP address is the IP address of Y
questions.to_find: Which statements about the destination IP and MAC addresses of the packet leaving X are TRUE

understand.plain: When a host sends a packet to a destination on a different subnet, the IP destination address always reflects the ultimate destination (Y), while the MAC destination address reflects the next-hop device (router R). Switches are transparent at layer 2 and do not appear as MAC destinations.

keywords.explain: End-to-end address identifying the final destination; does not change as the packet traverses routers (except in NAT scenarios).
keywords.example: A packet from 192.168.1.5 to 10.0.0.1 always has dst IP = 10.0.0.1.

keywords.explain: Link-layer address used hop-by-hop; it is the MAC of the next-hop router on the current segment, not the final destination.
keywords.example: Host sends packet with dst MAC = router's MAC, even though final destination is far away.

keywords.explain: A layer-2 device that forwards frames based on MAC addresses; it is transparent to the sender and does not have its own MAC as a destination in normal forwarding.
keywords.example: Switch S forwards the frame toward R; X never addresses the frame to S.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='500' height='160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='5' y='5' width='490' height='150' rx='8' class='bg'/><rect x='20' y='55' width='50' height='40' rx='4' class='hi'/><text x='45' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>X</text><rect x='130' y='55' width='50' height='40' rx='4' class='bg'/><text x='155' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>S</text><rect x='240' y='55' width='50' height='40' rx='4' class='bg'/><text x='265' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>R</text><rect x='390' y='55' width='50' height='40' rx='4' class='hi'/><text x='415' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Y</text><line x1='70' y1='75' x2='130' y2='75' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='180' y1='75' x2='240' y2='75' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='290' y1='75' x2='390' y2='75' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='6,3'/><text x='100' y='50' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Ethernet</text><text x='45' y='115' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>src</text><text x='415' y='115' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>dst</text><text x='230' y='130' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>dst IP = Y's IP | dst MAC = R's MAC</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Path: X -- (Ethernet with switch S) -- Router R -- (other network) -- Y. The frame leaving X has dst IP = Y's IP address and dst MAC = R's MAC address.

given.aim: Determine the destination IP address and destination MAC address in the packet as it leaves node X.

terms.meaning: An active end-to-end connection; the IP destination is always Y's IP for all packets in this connection.
terms.example: HTTP request from client X to server Y carries dst IP = Y.
terms.connects: Confirms the IP destination is Y's address, not any intermediate device.

terms.meaning: A layer-3 device; X's ARP resolves R's MAC as the next-hop gateway, so the frame's dst MAC = R's MAC.
terms.example: X sends an ARP for its default gateway (R) and gets R's MAC.
terms.connects: The dst MAC address in the frame leaving X is R's MAC.

terms.meaning: A layer-2 transparent forwarding device; it never appears as a destination MAC in normal operation.
terms.example: S just forwards frames without being a destination itself.
terms.connects: Eliminates option C (dst MAC = S's MAC) as incorrect.

given.plan: Apply the layered networking model: IP (layer 3) carries end-to-end addressing (X to Y), while Ethernet (layer 2) carries hop-by-hop addressing (X's MAC to R's MAC). Switches are transparent at layer 2.

steps.title: Determine destination IP address
steps.apply:
The TCP connection is between X and Y. The IP layer carries the source and destination addresses of the communicating endpoints. When X sends a packet to Y, dst IP = Y's IP address. It does NOT change to R's IP because that would break the end-to-end connection semantics.
- Option A (dst IP = R's IP): FALSE
- Option B (dst IP = Y's IP): TRUE
steps.note: IP addresses identify end hosts. Intermediate routers examine but do not become the destination IP.

steps.title: Determine destination MAC address
steps.formula_raw: dst_MAC = MAC(next_hop) = MAC(R)  [hop-by-hop at Ethernet layer]
steps.apply:
On the Ethernet segment from X to R (with switch S in between), X needs to deliver the frame to the next-hop router R. X uses ARP to find R's MAC address and sets dst MAC = R's MAC.
- Switch S is transparent (layer 2) -- X never sets dst MAC = S's MAC.
- Y is on a different subnet, so X cannot directly use Y's MAC.
- Option C (dst MAC = S's MAC): FALSE
- Option D (dst MAC = Y's MAC): FALSE
steps.note: The switch S simply forwards frames; it does not consume them. Dst MAC is always the next-hop router's MAC.

solution.result: Statement B is TRUE: The destination IP address is the IP address of Y.

questions.formula_note: IP addressing is end-to-end (Layer 3); MAC addressing is hop-by-hop (Layer 2). The destination MAC in a frame from X is always the MAC of the next-hop router, not the switch or final host.

questions.question: Consider an Ethernet segment with a transmission speed of 10^8 bits/sec and a maximum segment length of 500 meters. If the speed of propagation of the signal in the medium is >= 2 * 10^8 m/sec, then the minimum frame size (in bits) required for collision detection is ___________.
questions.to_find: Minimum frame size in bits required for collision detection in the given Ethernet segment

understand.plain: In CSMA/CD, a sender must still be transmitting when a collision signal returns to it. The worst case is when the collision happens at the far end of the segment. The frame must be large enough that its transmission time is at least twice the propagation delay (round-trip time). We calculate this minimum frame size.

keywords.explain: A medium access protocol where a sender listens while transmitting; if a collision is detected, transmission stops. The sender must detect the collision before finishing the frame.
keywords.example: If a collision happens at the far end, the signal returns in 2 * propagation_delay seconds.

keywords.explain: The smallest frame that ensures the sender is still transmitting when the collision echo returns. Frame transmission time >= 2 * propagation delay.
keywords.example: For 10 Mbps Ethernet with 2.5 km max length, minimum frame = 64 bytes.

keywords.explain: Time for a signal to travel from one end of the segment to the other: distance / propagation_speed.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='480' height='200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='5' y='5' width='470' height='190' rx='8' class='bg'/><rect x='20' y='70' width='50' height='40' rx='4' class='hi'/><text x='45' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>A</text><rect x='400' y='70' width='50' height='40' rx='4' class='bg'/><text x='425' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>B</text><line x1='70' y1='90' x2='400' y2='90' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/><text x='235' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>500 m segment</text><path d='M70,90 L200,130' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2' fill='none' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><text x='130' y='155' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>signal from A</text><path d='M400,90 L270,130' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2' fill='none'/><text x='340' y='155' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>collision echo</text><text x='235' y='185' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>min_frame_time = 2 * (500 / 2*10^8) = 5 us; min_bits = 5e-6 * 10^8 = 500 bits</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Ethernet segment of 500 m with node A on the left sending a frame. In the worst case, collision at node B returns signal to A. Round-trip propagation delay determines minimum frame size.

given.aim: Find the minimum frame size such that a sender detects any collision before it finishes transmitting.

terms.meaning: Rate at which bits are placed on the wire.
terms.example: A 500-bit frame takes 500 / 10^8 = 5 microseconds to transmit.
terms.connects: Used to convert time to number of bits: bits = time * speed.

terms.meaning: The farthest two nodes can be from each other on this Ethernet segment.
terms.example: A signal takes 500 / (2*10^8) = 2.5 us to reach the far end.

terms.meaning: Signal travel speed in the medium; using the minimum value (2 * 10^8 m/sec) gives the worst case (maximum propagation delay).
terms.connects: Maximum propagation delay = 2.5 us; worst-case round-trip = 5 us.

given.plan: Use the CSMA/CD constraint: frame_transmission_time >= 2 * propagation_delay. Calculate propagation delay using worst case (minimum propagation speed = 2 * 10^8 m/s), then compute minimum frame size = 2 * propagation_delay * transmission_rate.

steps.title: Calculate one-way propagation delay
steps.apply:
t_prop = 500 m / (2 * 10^8 m/s) = 2.5 * 10^-6 s = 2.5 us
(Using minimum propagation speed for worst-case maximum delay.)
steps.note: Worst case occurs when propagation speed is at its minimum, giving maximum delay.

steps.title: Calculate round-trip propagation delay
steps.apply:
RTT = 2 * 2.5 * 10^-6 s = 5 * 10^-6 s = 5 us
The frame transmission time must be >= RTT for the sender to still be transmitting when the collision echo arrives.
steps.note: The collision in worst case happens at the farthest point; echo must travel back the full 500 m.

steps.title: Calculate minimum frame size in bits
steps.note: This is the minimum number of bits the frame must contain so that transmission takes at least 5 us.

questions.formula_note: CSMA/CD minimum frame size rule: min_frame_size = 2 * (segment_length / propagation_speed) * transmission_rate = 2 * propagation_delay * bandwidth.

questions.question:
Which of the following statements is TRUE?

A. Both Ethernet frame and IP packet include checksum fields
B. Ethernet frame includes a checksum field and IP packet includes a CRC field
C. Ethernet frame includes a CRC field and IP packet includes a checksum field
D. Both Ethernet frame and IP packet include CRC fields
questions.answer: C. Ethernet frame includes a CRC field and IP packet includes a checksum field
questions.to_find: Which statement correctly describes the error detection fields in Ethernet frames and IP packets

understand.plain: Ethernet uses a 4-byte CRC (Cyclic Redundancy Check) field called FCS (Frame Check Sequence) at the end of each frame for error detection. IP packets, on the other hand, include a 16-bit ones-complement checksum in the header to detect errors in the IP header. These are two different error detection mechanisms.

keywords.explain: A polynomial-based error detection code appended to the frame; the receiver recomputes CRC and compares to detect transmission errors.
keywords.example: Ethernet FCS is a 32-bit CRC computed over the entire frame.

keywords.explain: A simpler error detection mechanism using addition of data words (ones complement for IP); covers only the IP header.
keywords.example: IP header checksum is 16-bit ones-complement sum of the header words.

keywords.explain: The 4-byte CRC field at the end of an Ethernet frame used for error detection at the data-link layer.
keywords.example: An Ethernet frame ends with: ... | Data | FCS (4 bytes) |

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='480' height='200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='5' y='5' width='470' height='190' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='235' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Ethernet Frame vs IP Packet Error Detection</text><rect x='10' y='45' width='220' height='30' rx='4' class='hi'/><text x='120' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Ethernet Frame</text><rect x='10' y='80' width='50' height='25' rx='2' class='bg'/><text x='35' y='97' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Preamble</text><rect x='62' y='80' width='50' height='25' rx='2' class='bg'/><text x='87' y='97' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Dst MAC</text><rect x='115' y='80' width='50' height='25' rx='2' class='bg'/><text x='140' y='97' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Src MAC</text><rect x='168' y='80' width='30' height='25' rx='2' class='bg'/><text x='183' y='97' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Type</text><rect x='10' y='110' width='180' height='25' rx='2' class='bg'/><text x='100' y='127' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Data (Payload)</text><rect x='192' y='110' width='38' height='25' rx='2' class='ok'/><text x='211' y='127' text-anchor='middle' class='sub' style='fill:white;'>FCS/CRC</text><rect x='245' y='45' width='220' height='30' rx='4' class='hi'/><text x='355' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>IP Packet Header</text><rect x='245' y='80' width='60' height='25' rx='2' class='bg'/><text x='275' y='97' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Version/IHL</text><rect x='307' y='80' width='60' height='25' rx='2' class='bg'/><text x='337' y='97' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>TTL/Proto</text><rect x='370' y='80' width='95' height='25' rx='2' class='ok'/><text x='417' y='97' text-anchor='middle' class='sub' style='fill:white;'>Header Checksum</text><text x='235' y='175' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Ethernet: CRC in FCS field | IP: ones-complement Checksum in header</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Side-by-side comparison: Ethernet frame ends with FCS (CRC) field highlighted in green; IP packet header contains a Header Checksum field highlighted in green. These are different error-detection mechanisms.

given.aim: Identify the correct error-detection field types used in Ethernet frames and IP packet headers.

terms.meaning: Frame Check Sequence: a 32-bit CRC appended to every Ethernet frame for link-layer error detection.
terms.example: IEEE 802.3 standard defines a 4-byte CRC-32 as the FCS of every Ethernet frame.
terms.connects: Confirms that Ethernet uses CRC, not a simple checksum.

terms.meaning: A 16-bit ones-complement checksum of the IP header fields only; computed and verified at each router.
terms.example: RFC 791 specifies the 16-bit ones-complement checksum in the IP header.
terms.connects: Confirms that IP uses a checksum, not CRC.

terms.meaning: Checksum is a simple arithmetic sum used in IP/TCP/UDP headers; CRC is a stronger polynomial-based check used in Ethernet, HDLC etc.
terms.example: IP checksum: sum all 16-bit words, take complement. Ethernet CRC: polynomial division.
terms.connects: These are distinct mechanisms; mixing them up is a common exam trap.

given.plan: Recall from protocol standards: IEEE 802.3 (Ethernet) uses CRC-32 in FCS; IPv4 (RFC 791) uses 16-bit ones-complement checksum in the header. Match to the options.

steps.title: Identify Ethernet frame error detection
steps.apply:
IEEE 802.3 Ethernet frame structure: Preamble (7B) | SFD (1B) | Dst MAC (6B) | Src MAC (6B) | EtherType (2B) | Payload | FCS (4B).
The FCS is a 32-bit CRC computed over the Ethernet frame. It is NOT a checksum.
Conclusion: Ethernet uses CRC.
steps.note: The term 'FCS' stands for Frame Check Sequence and uses CRC-32 polynomial.

steps.title: Identify IP packet error detection
steps.apply:
IPv4 header (RFC 791) contains a 16-bit 'Header Checksum' field. It is the ones-complement of the ones-complement sum of all 16-bit words in the header.
This is a checksum, NOT CRC.
Conclusion: IP uses checksum.
steps.note: The IP checksum only covers the header, not the data payload.

steps.title: Match to options
steps.apply:
A. Both use checksum: FALSE (Ethernet uses CRC)
B. Ethernet uses checksum, IP uses CRC: FALSE (both reversed)
C. Ethernet uses CRC, IP uses checksum: TRUE
D. Both use CRC: FALSE (IP uses checksum)
steps.note: Option C correctly states: Ethernet frame has CRC (FCS), IP packet has checksum.

solution.result: Option C is TRUE: Ethernet frame includes a CRC field and IP packet includes a checksum field.

questions.formula_note: Ethernet uses CRC-32 (FCS) for link-layer error detection; IPv4 uses a 16-bit ones-complement header checksum. These are distinct error-detection schemes defined in IEEE 802.3 and RFC 791 respectively.
